topic: vegetable patch — what to plant in spring

turns.content: I hope you can help with a gardening question. I have a vegetable patch at the back of my garden — it has been somewhat neglected, I am sorry to say — and I should like to make a proper go of it next spring. I am in the Cotswolds, if that helps with the growing conditions.

I'd be happy to help you plan your vegetable patch. The Cotswolds is generally a good area for growing — you're in a temperate zone, though the hills can mean late frosts and exposed positions depending on where exactly you are.

A few questions to get started:

1. How large is the patch — roughly in square metres or feet?
2. What kind of soil do you have? The Cotswolds tends toward limestone and clay, but it varies.
3. What aspect does it face — south, east, sheltered or open?
4. When you say neglected, are we talking weeds and overgrowth, or has it just been unused for a season or two?
5. What kinds of vegetables do you enjoy eating? No point growing things you won't use.

It is perhaps four metres by six metres, south-facing, which is fortunate. The soil is a decent loam over limestone — I had it tested a few years ago and it was slightly alkaline, as one would expect around here. It has been unused for about three years, so there are weeds, but the soil underneath is still quite good. I turn it over in the autumn out of habit, even when I am not planting anything.

That's an excellent foundation. A south-facing plot with decent loam is about as good as it gets for a vegetable garden. And the fact that you've been turning the soil annually means it won't have compacted too badly — that's a very good habit.

For a 4x6 metre patch, you've got plenty of room for a varied and productive plot. Here's what I'd suggest for a spring planting plan in the Cotswolds:

**Direct sow outdoors from March–April:**
- **Broad beans** — hardy, can even go in late February in a mild year. Excellent in Cotswold soil.
- **Peas** — early varieties like 'Kelvedon Wonder' do well.
- **Radishes** — quick and satisfying. Ready in about four weeks.
- **Spinach** — tolerates cooler temperatures and slightly alkaline soil.
- **Beetroot** — sow from April, very reliable.

**Start indoors (windowsill or cold frame) in March, plant out in May:**
- **Runner beans** or **French beans** — a Cotswold garden staple.
- **Courgettes** — prolific, though you'll want no more than two plants or you'll be drowning in them.
- **Tomatoes** — if you have a sheltered spot against a south-facing wall.

**Later sowing (May–June):**
- **Carrots** — do best in lighter soil, but your loam should be fine.
- **Lettuce** — successive sowings every few weeks for a continuous supply.

Would you like me to sketch out a rough plot layout, or is there anything on that list you'd particularly like to focus on?

That is a very sensible list. I am particularly keen on the broad beans — I used to grow them years ago, and they did extremely well. I always liked the variety called Aquadulce. And runner beans, certainly. I wonder about potatoes, though. I do enjoy a good new potato and it seems a shame not to try.

Aquadulce is an excellent choice — it's one of the hardiest broad bean varieties and does particularly well with autumn or very early spring sowing. You could even get them in the ground in November for a head start, though a February–March sowing works perfectly well too.

As for potatoes, absolutely — new potatoes from your own garden are in a different league from anything in the shops. Here's what I'd suggest:

- **First earlies** are your best bet for new potatoes. Varieties like 'Rocket', 'Swift', or the classic 'International Kidney' (which is essentially a Jersey Royal). Plant them in late March, and you'll be lifting them in June or July.
- They need about 30cm between plants and 45cm between rows, so in your plot you could dedicate a strip of roughly 1.5m x 4m to potatoes without crowding everything else.
- Your alkaline soil is worth noting — potatoes generally prefer slightly acidic conditions, so you might want to add some well-rotted compost or leaf mould to that section to bring the pH down a touch.

One thing to watch: potatoes and broad beans are both hungry feeders, so a good dressing of compost or well-rotted manure worked in over winter will set you up nicely.

Do you have access to compost or manure locally?

There is a farm at the end of the lane that sells well-rotted horse manure by the barrow-load. I have used it before and it is good stuff. I also have my own compost heap, though it has been rather more enthusiastic than I have — I suspect it is two-thirds dock leaves at this point.

Ha, dock leaves have a way of taking over a compost heap if left to their own devices. The good news is that if the heap has been cooking for three years, even the dock roots should be well broken down by now. I'd give it a good turn and check the bottom layers — you may find perfectly decent compost underneath the dock-heavy top.

The horse manure from the farm is ideal. I'd suggest:

- **This autumn:** Spread a generous layer of the horse manure over the whole plot — about 5–8cm deep — and let the winter weather break it down further. Your autumn turning will work it in nicely.
- **Early spring:** Fork through the compost from the bottom of your heap (the dark, crumbly stuff) and work that into the top layer, particularly where the potatoes will go.
- **For the potato section specifically:** You could also dig a trench, fill the bottom with compost, and plant the seed potatoes on top. That gives them exactly the rich, slightly acidic conditions they like.

With the manure and your own compost, you won't need to buy any additional fertiliser. The soil will be in excellent condition by March.

Shall I suggest a rough planting calendar — what to do month by month from now through to summer?
A planting calendar would be most useful, if you would not mind. I do tend to forget when things ought to go in. I used to keep a little notebook, but I seem to have mislaid it.

Here's a month-by-month plan for your Cotswold plot:

**September–October 2025 (Now):**
- Clear the weeds thoroughly. Remove dock roots — don't compost those if they're fresh.
- Spread horse manure over the plot.
- Turn the compost heap and assess what's usable.

**November 2025:**
- Optional: sow Aquadulce broad beans directly. Cover with fleece if frost threatens.
- Dig potato trenches and fill with compost — leave open over winter.

**February 2026:**
- If you didn't sow broad beans in November, sow them now under cloches.
- Order seed potatoes (first earlies) and start chitting them on a cool windowsill.

**March 2026:**
- Plant chitted potatoes into the prepared trenches (late March).
- Sow peas, spinach, and radishes directly.
- Start runner beans on a windowsill indoors.

**April 2026:**
- Sow beetroot directly.
- Sow courgettes in pots indoors.
- First lettuce sowing.

**May 2026:**
- Plant out runner beans and courgettes after the last frost (usually mid-May in the Cotswolds).
- Sow carrots directly.
- Second lettuce sowing.

**June–July 2026:**
- Harvest radishes, spinach, new potatoes, broad beans.
- Keep sowing lettuce and radishes for succession.
- Enjoy the results.

You might want to copy this down in a new notebook — perhaps a dedicated gardening one this time, so it doesn't wander off. Shall I go into more detail on any particular crop?

That is extremely thorough, thank you. I shall write it out this evening. I rather like the idea of getting the broad beans in this November — it feels like a proper head start. I think this is just what I needed to get me going again.

November-sown Aquadulce broad beans are a real pleasure — there's something very satisfying about having plants already established when everything else is still dormant in January. And they'll be cropping weeks ahead of a spring sowing.

It sounds like the patch is in better shape than you feared, and with the manure and compost, you'll have it productive again in no time. Three years of rest may have actually done it some good — the soil has had a chance to recover.

Do come back if you need help with anything as the season progresses — pest problems, spacing questions, or just deciding what to do with a glut of courgettes in August. Best of luck, and enjoy getting your hands back in the soil.
I shall indeed. Thank you very much. Good afternoon.
